# Limon Engine

> Open-source, LGPL-licensed, multi-platform 3D game engine (C++ and Python scripting) focused on ease of use, targeting integrated GPU hardware.

Limon Engine (v0.7) is a deliberately monolithic 3D game engine. It ships a built-in in-process level editor (Dear ImGui, no separate play mode), forward and deferred rendering pipelines, a node-based visual pipeline editor, CPU and GPU particle systems, 3D AI pathfinding, Bullet Physics, OpenAL audio, a layered GUI system, and a fully XML-configurable input system with first-class keyboard/mouse and gamepad support. Gameplay is extended via C++ plugins or Python scripts (pybind11) through five extension points: Actions, Player Extensions, AI Actors, Camera Attachments, and RenderMethods. All five types are loaded from a single user dynamic library at engine launch.

Supported platforms: Linux (primary), Windows, macOS (Apple Silicon), Raspberry Pi 4/5. The graphics backend is a swappable dynamic library — the engine ships OpenGL 3.3 and OpenGL ES 3.1. Custom backends (Vulkan, Metal, DirectX) are possible without modifying engine source.

## Rendering

**Pipeline editor.** The rendering pipeline is a node-based visual graph. Every available GLSL shader is discovered at launch via runtime reflection and appears as a node type automatically — including custom shaders placed in the shader directory. Nodes are wired together to define GPU resource flow; the Screen node is the terminal. The compiler automatically reorders passes for correctness based on the dependency graph. Two pipelines ship with the engine: forward (single-pass, lower memory) and deferred (lighting decoupled across passes, scales with many lights). The active pipeline is switchable at runtime via `changeRenderPipeline(pipelineFileName)`.

**RenderMethod — fifth extension point.** Each pipeline node selects a RenderMethod: a custom GPU rendering primitive loaded from the user dynamic library alongside all other extension types. A RenderMethod implements `initRender` / `renderFrame` / `cleanupRender` and exposes `GenericParameter` configuration that appears as editable fields directly on the node in the pipeline editor, persisted in both the node graph and the runtime pipeline. Two built-in RenderMethods ship: Render By Tag (primary geometry renderer — uses a named camera's culling results and a tag filter) and Quad Renderer (full-screen quad for post-processing passes, no geometry iteration). SSAO ships as a sample RenderMethod demonstrating GenericParameter configuration; its source is the recommended starting point for custom effects.

**Visibility filtering.** Five stages run in order per camera each frame, with each camera's workload on a separate thread:

1. **Tag filtering** — each camera and each scene object carries a tag; a pass specifies which camera tags render which object tags. Tags are uint128 hashes (zero-cost matching at runtime). The engine auto-tags objects as `animated`, `static`, `transparent`, and others. Custom pipeline configurations target specific tags to enable per-tag shaders or dedicated passes for object categories.
2. **Frustum culling** — geometry outside the view volume is discarded. Point lights use sphere-based culling instead: a frustum test would wrongly cull lights behind the camera that still illuminate visible geometry.
3. **Size-based full skip** — objects below a configurable projected screen-space size threshold are discarded entirely. Objects closer than `SkipRenderDistance` or larger than `MaxSkipRenderSize` are exempt. This is a binary skip, independent of LOD.
4. **SIMD software occlusion culling** — software depth buffer on the CPU. Objects above a configurable size threshold act as occluders; smaller objects are tested against the buffer. SSE4.1 on x86, NEON on AArch64 (Apple Silicon, Raspberry Pi 4/5).
5. **LOD selection** — automatic 4-level LOD per model (3 progressive simplifications plus the original mesh) generated via meshoptimizer. LOD mesh chosen based on projected screen-space size and the engine-wide `LodDistanceList`. Not configurable per model.

**Lighting and shadows.** Cascaded shadow maps for directional lights (4 cascades by default, with optional staggered rendering for ~10% performance gain). Cube map shadows for point lights. No global illumination — direct lighting with shadow maps only. Lights are creatable and removable at runtime via API. SSAO available as a sample post-processing RenderMethod. All materials are uploaded as a UBO with a per-instance material index; any shader declaring the UBO is detected automatically via reflection.

## Camera

All camera types are supported natively: first-person, third-person, top-down, orbital, isometric, and fixed, in both perspective and orthographic projection. Cameras are Camera Attachments — first-class scene objects. The engine ships a first-person default plus perspective and orthographic attachment samples.

## Physics

Bullet Physics with automatic collision shape generation: full mesh for static objects, convex hull for dynamic objects, per-bone convex hulls for animated objects, and UCX_-prefixed custom collision meshes.

## AI

A 3D navigation grid is built per world via flood fill. Route requests are asynchronous — the simulation tick is never blocked. Any extension can query the pathfinding system.

## Audio

OpenAL backend. OGG and WAV supported. Sounds can attach to scene objects and bones for 3D positional audio that follows geometry. Audio channel volumes configurable at runtime via API.

## Animation and Attachments

Skeletal animation with GPU-side skinning. Models, lights, particles, sounds, trigger volumes, and cameras can attach to specific named bones and follow them through the full skeletal animation.

## Editor

The editor runs inside the game process — no separate play mode. Changes apply to the next frame in a live world. Built with Dear ImGui.

**Player modes.** Four modes: Physical (normal gameplay with physics), Debug (no physics collision — can fly and walk through objects; renders physics meshes, GUI borders, and AI nav grid for debugging), Editor (authoring), and Menu (free cursor, movement disabled).

**Authoring tools.** Models, trigger volumes, lights, GUI elements, particles, and camera rigs are placed from the world properties panel. Translate/rotate/scale gizmos with configurable snap. Separate Object and GUI selection modes. Copy-with-offsets for rapid grid or wall creation. "On Top" toggle (shortcut T) on model objects: when checked, every move snaps the object down to the surface beneath it; search range is +1 to −10 Y from the object's current position. Useful for placing objects on uneven terrain. Session-level state, not saved per object. World object tree and asset browser both support case-insensitive text filtering (matching ancestors automatically shown). Asset browser shows model previews (lit by the current world's directional light) and texture hover previews. Material registry — global list of all world materials; per-mesh editing is either global (affects all users instantly) or per-model copy-on-write (permanent independent copy). Animation sequencer for keyframing any GameObject transform or GUI alpha. Node-based pipeline editor. AI actor type and Player Extension selected from auto-populated dropdowns (no manual name entry); their configurable parameters appear directly beneath.

**World properties.** OnLoad actions (multiple), level music (change/clear), skybox (6-image cubemap), loading image, ESC key behaviour (quit, return-previous world, or load-a-named-world).

**Release preparation.** "Convert models to binary" converts all world models to `.limonmodel` and updates the world file paths automatically. Navigation grid can be generated then saved as binary (fast-load, for shipping) or XML (human-readable debug format).

No undo/redo — planned for 0.8.

For architectural detail, API contracts, known limitations, and implementation notes: [llms-full.txt](llms-full.txt)

## Key Documentation

- [Engine Architecture](EngineArchitecture.html): System overview — game object types (Model, Model Group, Player, GUI Text/Button/Image, CPU/GPU Particle, Light, Sound, Camera Rig, Skybox, Trigger Volume), bone attachment rules, GenericParameter unified data contract, physics, AI, audio, GUI, platform support, and third-party libraries.
- [Rendering Pipeline](RenderingPipeline.html): Node-based visual pipeline editor, built-in shaders, RenderMethod extension point, SIMD occlusion culling, LOD, cascaded shadow maps, SSAO, and all rendering configuration options.
- [Supporting Systems](SupportingSystems.html): 3D navigation/pathfinding (async route requests), particle system API (CPU and GPU), debug line draw with per-vertex colour, in-game logger overlay, Tracy profiler integration with embedded flame graph.
- [C++ API Reference](ExtendingByAPI/CPPAPIReference.html): Full LimonAPI method signatures.
- [Python API Reference](ExtendingByAPI/pythonApi.html): Python scripting API (pybind11 bridge to LimonAPI).
- [Options Reference](OptionsReference.html): All engine options — display, rendering, shadows, SSAO, culling, LOD, player movement, audio volumes, debug/profiling. Options readable and writable at runtime via API.
- [Input System](ExtendingByAPI/InputSystem.html): XML-configurable action/binding system (keyboard, mouse, gamepad, raw joystick), custom game actions queryable by name hash in C++ and Python, gamepad dead zone, look speed alignment, active device detection.

## Extension Points

- [Implement Action](ExtendingByAPI/implementAction.html): Trigger, GUI button, and map-load callbacks — available in C++ and Python.
- [Implement Player Extension](ExtendingByAPI/implementPlayerExtension.html): Per-frame player input and movement control.
- [Implement AI Actor](ExtendingByAPI/implementAIActor.html): Per-simulation-step AI callbacks with async 3D pathfinding access and line-of-sight information.
- [Implement Camera Attachment](ExtendingByAPI/implementCameraAttachment.html): Custom camera types returning pose and projection (perspective or orthographic) each frame.
- [C++ API Usage](ExtendingByAPI/CPPAPIUsage.html): Plugin structure, GenericParameter, extension loading from a dynamic library.
- [Python API Usage](ExtendingByAPI/PythonAPIUsage.html): Writing gameplay scripts in Python.
- [API Fundamentals](ExtendingByAPI/APIFundamentals.html): GenericParameter unified data contract — one struct drives editor UI widget generation, serialisation, and extension configuration for all five extension points.

## Getting Started

- [Getting Started](GettingStarted.html): Prebuilt binaries (Windows/Linux/macOS/Raspberry Pi), build instructions, and dependencies.
- [Using the Editor](UsingTheEditor.html): In-process live editor, object placement, material editing, pipeline editor, animation sequencer, GUI authoring.
- [Asset Management](AssetManagement.html): limonmodel format, asset loading, materials, textures, animations, world file format.
- [Roadmap](RoadMap.html): Planned features and release targets.
